title: If proxy kubeconfig file exists ensure permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive

input_row.title: If proxy kubeconfig file exists ensure permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive
input_row.description: If kube-proxy is running, and if it is using a file-based kubeconfig file, ensure that the proxy kubeconfig file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.rationale: The kube-proxy kubeconfig file controls various parameters of the kube-proxy service in the worker node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. It is possible to run kube-proxy with the kubeconfig parameters configured as a Kubernetes ConfigMap instead of a file. In this case, there is no proxy kubeconfig file. Impact: None
input_row.audit: Find the kubeconfig file being used by kube-proxy by running the following command: ps -ef | grep kube-proxy If kube-proxy is running, get the kubeconfig file location from the --kubeconfig parameter. To perform the audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, stat -c %a <path><filename> Verify that a file is specified and it exists with permissions are 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, chmod 644 <proxy kubeconfig file> Default Value: By default, proxy file has permissions of 640. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-proxy/
input_row.profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Worker Node •  Level 2 - Worker Node

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- This control requires checking the file permissions of the kube-proxy's kubeconfig file on each worker node.
- The location of the kubeconfig file is determined by the --kubeconfig flag in the kube-proxy process, which is not exposed via the Kubernetes API or kubectl.
- File system permissions (e.g., 644 or more restrictive) are not queryable via Kubernetes APIs or kubectl; they require direct OS-level access.
- The audit instructions explicitly require running OS-level commands (e.g., stat, chmod) on each node.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. On each worker node, identify the kube-proxy process and determine if it is running:
   - Run: `ps -ef | grep kube-proxy`
2. If kube-proxy is running, identify the kubeconfig file path from the `--kubeconfig` flag in the process arguments.
3. Verify that the kubeconfig file exists at the specified path.
4. Check the file permissions of the kubeconfig file:
   - Run: `stat -c %a <path-to-kubeconfig-file>`
5. Confirm that the permissions are 644 or more restrictive (i.e., 644, 640, 600, etc.).
6. If the file permissions are more permissive than 644, remediate by running:
   - `chmod 644 <path-to-kubeconfig-file>`
7. Repeat these steps for every worker node in the cluster.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.7.1 PDF
